title: Workbook — Divine Revelation
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

1. Remembering the Essentials

  1. What does the word «revelation» mean, and why is it important that God takes the initiative in revealing himself?
  2. Name the three main stages of God's revelation to Israel that the class points out. Give one key event for each.
  3. Why is Jesus Christ the full and definitive revelation of God, according to the Catechism (CCC 65)? What makes him different from all the prophets?
  4. What are the three channels of transmission of revelation? Explain each one briefly and how they relate to one another.
  5. What does the Catechism mean when it affirms that «no new public revelation is to be expected» (CCC 66)? Does this mean God stopped speaking?
  6. Explain the relationship between Sacred Scripture and Sacred Tradition. Why can't we have one without the other (CCC 80-83)?
  7. What is the Magisterium, and what is its role in interpreting God's Word (CCC 85-87, 100)?
  8. What are the four senses of Scripture according to CCC 115-119? Name and briefly explain each one.

2. For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no «right» answers to these personal questions.

1. Have you ever had a «burning bush» experience in your life? A moment when you felt God was speaking to you in a special way.

Describe briefly when it was and what you felt.

___________ ___________ ___________ ___________ ___________

2. If God reveals himself gradually, what stage would you say you are living in right now?

Are you in a time of searching, of encounter, of crisis, of deepening? What makes you say that?

3. Do you find it hard to believe that Jesus is God himself, or does it come naturally to you?

Reflect on which aspects of Jesus' person you find easier or harder to accept as God's revelation. Why do you think that is?

4. What is your relationship with the Bible? Do you read it as God's Word or do you find it hard to see it that way?

What would change in your daily life if you truly believed that God speaks to you through its pages — today, right now?

5. Do you know someone who, like Saint Paul, had a radical conversion?

Or in your own life: have you experienced a deep change in how you see God? What triggered it?

6. The Catechism says that «the Church has always venerated the Scriptures as she venerates the Lord's Body» (CCC 103). What does this comparison between the Bible and the Eucharist tell you?

Does it change how you might approach reading Scripture?

7. Tradition is not a dead museum but a living stream. What traditions in the Church have helped you encounter God? (a particular prayer, a feast day, a family custom, a devotion...)

Is there a tradition you'd like to rediscover or learn more about?

3. Practical Activities

A. Prayerful Reading of Exodus 3

Read Exodus 3:1-15 in your Bible. Then sit in silence for five minutes and imagine you are Moses. What do you see? What do you feel? What does God say to you? Write down what you «hear» in your heart.

 

 

 

 

 

B. The Timeline of Revelation

Draw a horizontal line on a large sheet of paper. Mark the following moments: Adam and Eve, Noah, Abraham, Moses and the Covenant at Sinai, the prophets, Jesus Christ, the apostolic Church, today (you). Write next to each one what God reveals at that moment. Reflect: the line continues. You are on it. Where do you think God is leading you next?

C. Dialogue with Saint Paul

Read the account of Saint Paul's conversion in Acts 9:1-19 (or 22:1-21). Imagine you are a journalist interviewing Paul shortly after his encounter with Christ. Write five questions you would ask him, and then answer them as you think he would respond.

 

 

 

 

 

D. Lectio Divina — Your First Attempt

Try the ancient practice of lectio divina (sacred reading) this week. Pick a short passage (try Mark 1:9-11, the Baptism of Jesus). Follow these four steps:

  1. Lectio (Read): Read the passage slowly, twice. What word or phrase catches your attention?
  2. Meditatio (Meditate): Why does that word or phrase stand out? What might God be saying to you through it?
  3. Oratio (Pray): Speak to God about what you've read. Tell him what's in your heart.
  4. Contemplatio (Contemplate): Be still. Rest in God's presence. Don't try to figure anything out. Just be with him.

Write a short summary of your lectio divina experience here:

 

 

 

 

 

4. Verse to Memorize

«Long ago God spoke to our ancestors in many and various ways by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by a Son.»

— Hebrews 1:1-2

Going deeper: Also read John 1:1-18 (the Prologue). Memorize verse 14: «And the Word became flesh and lived among us, and we have seen his glory.» Write it here when ready:

 

 

5. Prayer for This Week

Lord, you who spoke to Moses from the burning bush,
and to Saint Paul on the road to Damascus,
speak to me as well.
Open my ears to hear your voice,
my eyes to recognize your passing,
and my heart to answer like Moses: «Here I am.»
You who are the eternal Word of the Father,
teach me to know you and love you.
Give me a hunger for your Scripture
and a trust in the Church you founded.
Amen.

Prayer with Scripture: This week, before you scroll on your phone or check social media, open your Bible app or a physical Bible for 3 minutes first. Read one short passage. Write here which passages you read each day:

 

 

 

6. Commitment for the Week

Choose at least one concrete commitment to live out this week. Check the one you choose:

I will try lectio divina (Activity D) at least twice this week with a different short passage each time.

I will read one chapter of a Gospel each day. Start with Mark (the shortest). That's about 5 minutes a day.

I will ask a priest, catechist, or knowledgeable friend to explain something about the Bible or Tradition that I've never understood.

I will pay attention during the Liturgy of the Word at Mass this Sunday. Write down the readings beforehand (check the missal or an app) and read them before Mass.

I will watch or read a testimony of someone who went from atheism or indifference to faith. Write down what struck you most.

My personal commitment (write it in your own words if you want something different):

 

 

At the end of the week, review: Did I keep my commitment? What changed in me by doing it? If I didn't keep it, what got in the way?

 

 

Print Workbook
